questions.retrievalTag: GAD excessive worry finances normal TSH buspirone
questions.reviewPearl: Buspirone (a 5-HT1A receptor partial agonist) is an effective maintenance therapy for GAD, particularly useful for avoiding the sexual dysfunction and weight gain associated with SSRIs.
questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman comes to the physician at her husband’s request because of increasing irritability and tense mood over the past year. Her husband would like to start a family and he is concerned about her ability to handle the added responsibility. She says that she worries about her finances despite the fact that they have no debt and she and her husband have jobs that pay well. Her pulse is 84/min, and blood pressure is 120/70 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities except for sweaty palms. Mental status examination shows tension and anxiety. She admits to occasional difficulty sleeping and periods of mild depression but does not think that this is out of ordinary. Laboratory studies show: Hematocrit 38% Erythrocyte count 4 million/mm3 Leukocyte count 5500/mm3 Serum Thyroid-stimulating hormone 0.5 µU/mL Thyroxine (T4) 12 µg/dL Toxicology screening is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify GAD and select buspirone as an appropriate pharmacotherapy.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient exhibits Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD), marked by excessive, irrational worry (finances) lasting >6 months, irritability, sleep difficulty, and physical symptoms (sweaty palms). Buspirone is a non-sedating anxiolytic indicated for GAD.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Her thyroid labs are normal, ruling out levothyroxine (C) and methimazole (D). Bupropion (A) is an activating antidepressant that can exacerbate anxiety. Quetiapine (E) is an antipsychotic not used first-line here.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Buspirone (a 5-HT1A receptor partial agonist) is an effective maintenance therapy for GAD, particularly useful for avoiding the sexual dysfunction and weight gain associated with SSRIs.

questions.retrievalTag: Normal adolescent development healthy weight reassure
questions.reviewPearl: Normal adolescent development frequently includes an increased need for privacy, minor oppositional behavior (eye-rolling), and diet experimentation, which should be reassured if growth and functioning remain stable.
questions.stem: A 13-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her father for a well-child examination. He is concerned about her weight and eating habits. He says that she is always “on the go” and never sits down for a full meal. She will often just grab a piece of fruit when she is on her way to an activity. She will eat a full meal when the family has dinner together every Sunday. She has talked about wanting to become a vegetarian because of her concern for animals. She has had the same group of friends since elementary school. She is the captain of her soccer team and practices 4 days weekly. She is active in an after-school drama program 3 days weekly and is the lead in this season’s play. She maintains a B grade average. Her father says that she talks on the telephone “constantly” and is animated and cheerful most of the time. When she is in her bedroom. She prefers to keep the door closed and stops talking if she is on the telephone and her father walks into the room. She has a disorganized bedroom and often falls fast asleep on a bed covered with piles of clothes. Menarche has not occurred. She appears thin and muscular. She is at the 50th percentile for height and 35th percentile for weight, which is unchanged from last year. Vital signs are within normal limits. Breast and pubic hair development are Tanner stage 2. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. When interviewed alone, she is animated. She shrugs and rolls her eyes when asked her diet and weight. She thinks she is fine and does not understand why her father is so upset. Which of the following is most appropriate next step?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Reassure the father that this is normal development

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Recommend that the father keep a log of what his daughter eats

questions.educationalObjective: Distinguish normal adolescent psychosocial development from eating disorders.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient's behaviors—desiring privacy, exploring vegetarianism, eating on the go, rolling eyes—are normal features of early adolescence. Her height and weight percentiles are stable, and her physical exam is normal, ruling out an eating disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Psychotherapy (B), nutritional counseling (C), logging food (D), and weekly weigh-ins (E) unnecessarily pathologize normal teenage development and could inadvertently trigger eating anxieties.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Normal adolescent development frequently includes an increased need for privacy, minor oppositional behavior (eye-rolling), and diet experimentation, which should be reassured if growth and functioning remain stable.

questions.retrievalTag: PTSD fear of flying CBT exposure
questions.reviewPearl: Avoidance is a core symptom of PTSD; exposure-based CBT directly addresses and extinguishes these maladaptive avoidance behaviors.
questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman comes to the physician for a follow-up examination 6 months after she was a passenger on an airplane that skidded off the runway into the harbor. Two people were killed; she escaped without serious injury. After the crash, she had insomnia, a depressed mood, nightmares about plan crashes, and daytime flashbacks that have been treated with clonazepam and paroxetine. Overall, her symptoms have improved, but she is still too anxious to fly. She works as a buyer for a large department store and was told by her manager that her job is in jeopardy because she is required to travel to Europe monthly. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows mild anxiety. Her thoughts are organized and goal directed. She has difficulty completing serial seven subtraction. In addition to continuing clonazepam and paroxetine therapy, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Recommend that the patient should switch to a job that does not require air travel

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Add quetiapine to the current regimen

questions.educationalObjective: Recognize that CBT (exposure therapy) is the treatment of choice for phobic avoidance in PTSD.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has PTSD with a specific phobic avoidance (fear of flying) that severely impacts her occupation. Trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) with exposure therapy is the first-line non-pharmacologic treatment to extinguish specific avoidance behaviors.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Telling her to switch jobs (A) reinforces maladaptive avoidance. Group (C) and psychodynamic (D) therapies are less targeted for specific phobias/avoidance than CBT. Quetiapine (E) is unnecessary polypharmacy.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Avoidance is a core symptom of PTSD; exposure-based CBT directly addresses and extinguishes these maladaptive avoidance behaviors.

questions.retrievalTag: Delayed sleep phase circadian rhythm weekends
questions.reviewPearl: Patients with delayed sleep phase syndrome have a shifted biological clock but normal sleep architecture; they sleep well when allowed to adhere to their preferred schedule.
questions.stem: A 27-year-old teacher comes to the physician requesting a prescription for sleep medication. She has been unable to fall asleep until 2 or 3 am since the start of the school year 3 months ago. When she awakens at 6 am, she feels drowsy and remains tired throughout the morning. Last week, she fell asleep at her desk while her students were watching a movie. She believes that her job is in jeopardy. Drinking one glass of wine before going to bed has not helped her fall asleep. She recently took one of her mother’s zolpidem tablets; she only slept for 2 hours and was then awaken until 3 or 4 am. During vacations and on weekends, she typically sleeps from 3 am until noon and feels energetic and well rested. She currently does not take any medications or drink alcohol. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she appears tired and is teary. She can recall two of three objects after 5 minutes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Differentiate delayed sleep phase disorder from primary insomnia by evaluating sleep quality on weekends/vacations.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: This is circadian rhythm sleep-wake disorder, delayed sleep phase type. When allowed to sleep on her own schedule (3 AM to noon), she gets restorative, normal sleep, distinguishing it from general insomnia.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Primary insomnia (F) would involve poor sleep regardless of the schedule. MDD (D) and Adjustment disorder (A) are incorrect because her tearfulness is secondary to sleep deprivation. Narcolepsy (E) involves sleep attacks, not a shifted circadian phase.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Patients with delayed sleep phase syndrome have a shifted biological clock but normal sleep architecture; they sleep well when allowed to adhere to their preferred schedule.

questions.retrievalTag: Chronic pelvic pain negative laparoscopies psychiatric assessment
questions.reviewPearl: To prevent iatrogenic injury, stop ordering invasive diagnostic procedures in patients with recurrent physical complaints who have already had extensive, negative evaluations.
questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman, gravida 1, para 1, comes to the physician because of a 5-month history of increasingly severe episodes of lower abdominal pain. The pain is exacerbated during bowel movements, sexual intercourse, and menses. Treatment with several analgesics has not controlled the pain. Laparoscopy 4 years ago and a second laparoscopy 2 years ago for evaluation of pain showed no abnormalities. She is sexually active with one male partner, and they use condoms for contraception. She is a single parent and lost her job 6 months ago. She now has difficulty sleeping 1 to 3 nights each week because she worries about finding a job. Abdominal examination shows tenderness in both lower quadrants. Pelvic examination shows a mobile, nontender uterus and normal, nontender adnexa. A complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, Pap smear, and urine and cervical cultures show no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to determine the cause of this patient’s pain?
questions.educationalObjective: Prioritize psychiatric evaluation in patients with chronic pain and exhaustive negative medical workups.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has chronic pelvic pain with an extensive negative medical workup (two prior negative laparoscopies, normal labs, normal pelvic exam) and concurrent psychosocial stressors. Psychiatric assessment is needed to evaluate for somatic symptom disorder or psychological factors affecting a medical condition.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Further invasive testing like hysteroscopy (C), laparoscopy (D), or endometrial biopsy (E) are unindicated and risk iatrogenic harm. MRI (A) is unnecessary given the previously definitively negative structural evaluations.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: To prevent iatrogenic injury, stop ordering invasive diagnostic procedures in patients with recurrent physical complaints who have already had extensive, negative evaluations.

questions.retrievalTag: Acute situational anxiety insomnia clonazepam breast cancer
questions.reviewPearl: Short courses of benzodiazepines are highly effective and appropriate for severe, acute distress and insomnia following a major stressor, provided they are strictly time-limited.
questions.stem: A 57-year-old woman comes to the physician because of difficulty sleeping, tearfulness, and restlessness since her daughter was diagnosed with metastatic breast cancer 3 days ago. She reports that when she goes to bed at night, she is unable to fall asleep for several hours and lays in bed worrying her daughter’s situation. The patient underwent a mastectomy for breast cancer 7 years ago. She takes acetaminophen/butalbital for occasional migraines. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she is tearful and tense but calms during conversation. There is no evidence of suicidal ideation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify the role of short-term benzodiazepines in treating severe acute situational anxiety and insomnia.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is experiencing severe, acute situational anxiety and insomnia triggered by her daughter's recent cancer diagnosis (a trauma echoing her own history). A short-term prescription of a benzodiazepine (clonazepam) is appropriate for rapid relief of severe acute stress-related insomnia.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: SSRIs like sertraline (H) take weeks to become effective. Imipramine (E), carbamazepine (B), and olanzapine (F) are inappropriate for simple acute situational distress. Pentobarbital (G) is dangerous and obsolete for insomnia.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Short courses of benzodiazepines are highly effective and appropriate for severe, acute distress and insomnia following a major stressor, provided they are strictly time-limited.

questions.retrievalTag: Schizophrenia prodrome negative symptoms timeline horses
questions.reviewPearl: A sudden psychotic break is often preceded by years of subtle negative symptoms (social withdrawal, academic/occupational decline), which counts toward the 6-month diagnostic criteria for schizophrenia.
questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman is brought to the physician by the owner of the stable where she works because she has been talking to the horses for 7 days. She dropped out of high school at age of 17 years and has worked at the stable since that time. She lives alone and has no friends. On questioning, she states that the horses tell her how to lead her life and that they have been given special powers by a god. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize that the >6-month timeline required for schizophrenia includes prodromal and residual phases, not just active psychosis.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: While her florid psychosis (auditory hallucinations/delusions) has only been present for 7 days, she has an 8-year history of functional decline and profound social isolation (negative/prodromal symptoms) since age 17. Because total symptom duration (including prodromal phase) exceeds 6 months, the diagnosis is schizophrenia.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Delusional disorder (C) lacks prominent hallucinations like talking to horses. Schizoid PD (D) features isolation but not acute psychosis. Brief psychotic disorder requires symptoms <1 month without the lengthy prodromal functional decline.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: A sudden psychotic break is often preceded by years of subtle negative symptoms (social withdrawal, academic/occupational decline), which counts toward the 6-month diagnostic criteria for schizophrenia.

questions.retrievalTag: Fetal alcohol syndrome smooth philtrum growth retardation
questions.reviewPearl: The defining facial features of FAS are a smooth philtrum, thin vermilion border (upper lip), and short palpebral fissures.
questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the physician by his adoptive parents because of ongoing concern about his behavior and academic performance. He is two grade levels behind his peers in reading and frequently has flights with other children. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. His biological mother abused multiple substances before and during pregnancy. On adoption at age of 15 months, there was some evidence that she may have physically abused him. He is at the 10th percentile for height and 5th percentile for weight. His pulse is 82/min, and blood pressure is 116/78 mm Hg. Examination shows a flattened nasal bridge, a long philtrum, and prominent ears. During the interview, he is friendly but becomes frustrated and distracted and is unable to sit still. Which of the following is most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify the hallmark physical and neurodevelopmental signs of Fetal Alcohol Syndrome.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient presents with the classic triad of Fetal Alcohol Syndrome (FAS): growth restriction (<10th percentile), CNS neurodevelopmental deficits (ADHD-like behavior, academic delay), and characteristic facial dysmorphology (smooth/long philtrum, flattened nasal bridge).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Fragile X syndrome (C) causes intellectual disability but features a long face, large ears, and macroorchidism, without the specific philtrum changes. Angelman (A) is characterized by severe intellectual disability, ataxia, and inappropriate laughter.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: The defining facial features of FAS are a smooth philtrum, thin vermilion border (upper lip), and short palpebral fissures.

questions.retrievalTag: Panic disorder recurrent attacks behavioral avoidance quitting football
questions.reviewPearl: The diagnosis of panic disorder requires not just the attacks themselves, but at least 1 month of persistent worry about future attacks or a maladaptive change in behavior to avoid them.
questions.stem: A 17-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother because of a 20-minute episode of severe chest pain and sweating that started 1 hour ago while he was watching television. On arrival, he is alert and repeatedly expresses concern that something is “very wrong.” He reports that during the episode, his heart was “pounding,” he felt short of breath, and he was sure he was having a heart attack and was going to die. One month ago, he had a similar episode that occurred in the evening approximately 30 minutes after his high school football practice. At that time, results of laboratory studies and an ECG obtained in the emergency department showed no abnormalities. After the initial episode, he quit the football team, and he was continued to limit his physical activity because he is afraid of bringing on another episode. He reports no insomnia or changes in appetite. His mother states that he was diagnosed with an innocent heart murmur at the age of 6 months, but it has not been noted on examinations since the age of 1 year. His paternal grandfather died of a myocardial infarction 3 years ago at the age of 68 years. The patient takes no medications. He does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. He performs well in school. He is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 73 kg (161 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 98/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 130/83 mm Hg. Cardiac examination shows a normal S1 and S2 with variation of S2 on respiration. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose panic disorder based on recurrent unexpected attacks and subsequent avoidance behaviors.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has experienced recurrent, unexpected panic attacks (palpitations, sweating, fear of dying) followed by >1 month of maladaptive behavioral changes (quitting football to avoid triggering another attack), diagnosing panic disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: A normal ECG and exam rule out structural heart disease like HCM (D) and pericarditis (E). GAD (C) features chronic worry rather than acute episodic panic. Acute stress disorder (A) requires a preceding specific severe traumatic event.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: The diagnosis of panic disorder requires not just the attacks themselves, but at least 1 month of persistent worry about future attacks or a maladaptive change in behavior to avoid them.

questions.retrievalTag: 2-month history crying spells hypersomnia weight gain MDD
questions.reviewPearl: When a patient meets the full criteria for a Major Depressive Episode, the diagnosis is Major Depressive Disorder, regardless of whether a clear precipitating stressor (like a recent breakup) is identified.
questions.stem: A previously healthy 27-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-month history of crying spells, poor concentration, fatigue, and lethargy. She says that she sleeps at least 12 hours daily and is no longer interested in socializing. She recently broke up with her boyfriend of 10 months, and her work performance has begun to decline; she is afraid that she will lose her job if it does not improve. She does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. Menses occur at regular 28- to 30-day intervals. Her last menstrual period was 2 weeks ago. She has had 9-kg (20-lb) weight gain over the past 9 months. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 66 kg (145 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows a sad mood and restricted affect. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose Major Depressive Disorder by identifying 5 or more symptoms of depression (SIGECAPS) lasting at least 2 weeks, recognizing that an MDD diagnosis supersedes Adjustment Disorder even when a clear psychosocial stressor is present.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: This patient presents with multiple SIGECAPS symptoms (depressed mood, hypersomnia, anhedonia, fatigue, poor concentration, and weight gain) that have been present for 2 months. This clinical picture fully satisfies the diagnostic criteria for Major Depressive Disorder (MDD), which requires at least 5 symptoms (including depressed mood or anhedonia) lasting for 2 or more weeks.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Adjustment disorder (A) is triggered by a stressor but cannot be diagnosed if the symptoms meet the criteria for another major psychiatric disorder, such as MDD. Dysthymic disorder (B) requires chronic depressive symptoms lasting for at least 2 years. Generalized anxiety disorder (C) is characterized by excessive worry rather than dominant depressive symptoms. Premenstrual dysphoric disorder (E) requires symptoms to arise in the final week before menses and resolve shortly after; her last menses was 2 weeks ago.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: When a patient meets the full criteria for a Major Depressive Episode, the diagnosis is Major Depressive Disorder, regardless of whether a clear precipitating stressor (like a recent breakup) is identified.

questions.retrievalTag: MDD pregnancy risks benefits antidepressants
questions.reviewPearl: Antidepressants can and should be used during pregnancy when the maternal and fetal risks of untreated severe depression outweigh the risks of medication exposure.
questions.stem: A 25-year-old primigravid woman at 16 weeks’ gestation has had increased episodes of crying, difficulty falling asleep, decreased energy, and loss of appetite over the past 6 weeks. She has a history of recurrent major depressive disorder. Her family is concerned that she might in inadvertently harm herself or her fetus. Her husband reports that she has lost interest in daily activities. She states that she has no suicidal thoughts. She is concerned about her ability to take care of a newborn and is questioning her decision not to terminate the pregnancy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Reassurance with return visit in 2 weeks

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Explanation of the risks and benefits of antidepressant therapy

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Explanation of the risks and benefits of therapeutic abortion

questions.educationalObjective: Manage severe major depressive disorder in a pregnant patient by counseling on the risks and benefits of antidepressant therapy.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has severe recurrent major depressive disorder during pregnancy. Given the risk that untreated severe depression poses to both the mother and fetus, the first-line management is to discuss the risks and benefits of initiating antidepressant therapy.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Reassurance (A) or just support groups (D) are insufficient for an active severe depressive episode. Hospital admission (E) is unnecessary as she currently denies active suicidal ideation.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Antidepressants can and should be used during pregnancy when the maternal and fetal risks of untreated severe depression outweigh the risks of medication exposure.

questions.retrievalTag: Depressed mood loud snoring high BMI OSA
questions.reviewPearl: Always rule out underlying medical conditions like Obstructive Sleep Apnea in obese patients presenting with chronic fatigue and depressive symptoms.
questions.stem: For each patient with depressed mood, select the most likely diagnosis

answerChoices.label: G
answerChoices.text: Mood disorder due to a general medical condition

answerChoices.label: H
answerChoices.text:
Substance-induced mood disorder
A previously healthy 37-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of depressed mood, fatigue, poor concentration, and difficulty sleeping. He works as an accountant and has had difficulty staying awake at work, particularly in the afternoon. He says that his wife sleeps in a separate room because of his loud snoring. He drinks alcohol at social functions only. He is 175 cm (5 ft 9 in) tall and weighs 109 kg (240 lb); BMI is 35 kg/m2. Physical examination shows no other abnormalities. Mental status examination shows a mildly depressed mood.

questions.educationalObjective: Identify secondary causes of depression, such as obstructive sleep apnea, in patients with high BMI and severe daytime somnolence.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient's depressed mood, daytime fatigue, loud snoring, and obesity (BMI 35) strongly point to obstructive sleep apnea (OSA). Mood symptoms secondary to untreated sleep apnea fall under a mood disorder due to a general medical condition.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: While he has depressive symptoms, the underlying etiology is physiological (apnea), making F (Major Depressive Disorder) incorrect. The symptoms are not tied to a specific stressor (A) or substances (H).

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Always rule out underlying medical conditions like Obstructive Sleep Apnea in obese patients presenting with chronic fatigue and depressive symptoms.

questions.retrievalTag: 1 week breakup tearful isolated adjustment disorder
questions.reviewPearl: Adjustment disorder features clinically significant emotional distress in response to a stressor occurring within 3 months, but the diagnosis is superseded if criteria for another specific disorder (like MDD) are met.
questions.stem: For each patient with depressed mood, select the most likely diagnosis

answerChoices.label: G
answerChoices.text: Mood disorder due to a general medical condition

answerChoices.label: H
answerChoices.text:
Substance-induced mood disorder
A 23-year-old graduate student comes to the physician because of depressed mood and feelings of hopelessness since breaking up with her boyfriend 1 week ago. During this period, she has rarely left her apartment, has only eaten a small amount of food, and has isolated herself from her friends and family. She has been drinking more alcohol than normal during this period. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she becomes tearful when she describes the breakup. When asked about her mood, she says, "I'm miserable. He was my life!" There is no evidence of suicidal ideation.

questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose Adjustment Disorder when mood symptoms occur out of proportion to a psychosocial stressor and do not meet full criteria for Major Depressive Disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has significant depressive symptoms causing functional impairment that began specifically in response to an identifiable stressor (breakup) just 1 week ago. Because symptoms have not lasted at least 2 weeks, she does not yet meet the criteria for MDD.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: F (MDD) is incorrect because symptoms have only been present for 1 week (2 weeks required). B (Bereavement) applies to the death of a loved one, not a breakup.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Adjustment disorder features clinically significant emotional distress in response to a stressor occurring within 3 months, but the diagnosis is superseded if criteria for another specific disorder (like MDD) are met.

questions.retrievalTag: 1 week post trauma police officer debriefing education
questions.reviewPearl: Psychological debriefing immediately following a trauma is no longer recommended due to evidence that it may worsen outcomes; providing education on normal trauma reactions is preferred.
questions.stem: A 37-year-old male police officer comes to the physician at the request of his superiors 1 week after he witnessed a terrorist bombing during which several civilians and three fellow police officers were killed. He sustained only minor injuries and assisted in rescuing survivors and gathering body parts. Since the bombing, he has felt emotionally numb and has been unable to enjoy activities he used to find pleasurable. He has continued to work but has requested assignments far removed from the site of the attack. He describes his sleep as fitful. Two years ago, he sustained a bullet wound to the right calf, which left him with a slight limp. He has no other history of medical or psychiatric illness. He says he used to drink one to two beers nightly but has been drinking two to three beers nightly for the past week. Physical examination confirms the previous gunshot injury to the calf and shows no other abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is irritable and says he is not depressed. He tells the physician, "They made me come. I'm not interested in talking with anyone. I just want to spend time with my buddies and be left alone." Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Encourage the patient to discuss the trauma in detail

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Provide information about the range of reactions to trauma

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Recommend group therapy with other trauma survivors

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Recommend a 1-month medical leave of absence from active duty

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Recommend a physician-led trauma debriefing series

questions.educationalObjective: Recognize that psychoeducation and support, rather than forced debriefing, are the most appropriate initial management steps for acute trauma responses.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is experiencing acute stress symptoms 1 week after a severe trauma. The standard of care in the acute aftermath of trauma is providing supportive care and psychoeducation regarding the normal range of stress reactions.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Forced trauma debriefing or making him discuss the trauma in detail (A, E) are contraindicated as they can interfere with natural coping mechanisms and actually increase the risk of PTSD. Medications (G, H) are not indicated 1 week post-trauma.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Psychological debriefing immediately following a trauma is no longer recommended due to evidence that it may worsen outcomes; providing education on normal trauma reactions is preferred.

questions.retrievalTag: Preoccupation multiple sclerosis mole cancer hypochondriasis
questions.reviewPearl:
In Illness Anxiety Disorder, the primary dysfunction is an overwhelming anxiety regarding the
meaning
of physical symptoms, not the severity of the symptoms themselves.
questions.stem: A 21-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 3-week history of muscle aches and fatigue associated with exercise. He is worried that he might have multiple sclerosis. Two months ago, he was concerned that a mole on his right arm may be cancerous. The physician reassured him that the mole was nonmalignant. One month ago, he had lateral chest pain while jogging. An ECG at that time showed no abnormalities. He appeared relieved once he heard the results. Today, his temperature is 36.7°C (98°F), pulse is 70/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 130/84 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he appears tense but makes full eye contact and is cooperative. He spears clearly and at a normal rate, and his thought process is goal directed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Differentiate hypochondriasis (illness anxiety disorder) from other somatic symptom disorders based on the primary fear of having a severe disease.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has an excessive fear of having a serious, life-threatening illness based on misinterpretation of mild or normal bodily symptoms (e.g., exercise fatigue as MS, a mole as cancer) despite repeated medical evaluation and reassurance. (Note: Under modern DSM-5 criteria, this aligns with Illness Anxiety Disorder).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Conversion disorder (A) involves specific neurological deficits. Delusional disorder (B) involves fixed false beliefs, but this patient's fears are responsive to reassurance (he was relieved after the ECG). Somatization disorder (E) requires multiple specific pain, GI, sexual, and pseudoneurological complaints over years.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body:
In Illness Anxiety Disorder, the primary dysfunction is an overwhelming anxiety regarding the
meaning
of physical symptoms, not the severity of the symptoms themselves.

questions.retrievalTag: 2 year old day care crying settles down normal reaction
questions.reviewPearl: Normal separation anxiety peaks around 18-24 months and is characterized by rapid calming and engagement in activities once the primary caregiver is out of sight.
questions.stem: A 2½-year-old boy is brought by his parents for a well-child examination. He recently started attending day care. The father reports that his son protests and hangs on his coat sleeves each morning so the day-care provider cannot take him. He sometimes says he has a stomachache. The day-care provider reports that after the father drives away, the child settles down and plays happily with other children. He eats well and takes a 90-minute nap. When his mother comes to pick him up, he whines, stamps his feet, clings to the day-care provider, and demands cookies. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Distinguish developmentally normal separation protest from pathological separation anxiety disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Protest at separation (clinging, crying) is a normal developmental stage for a toddler. Crucially, the child quickly calms down and functions normally (playing, eating, napping) after the parent departs, ruling out a pathological disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Separation anxiety disorder (K) involves severe, excessive anxiety that does not resolve quickly after the parent leaves and impairs overall functioning. Adjustment disorder (A) requires an excessive response to a stressor.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Normal separation anxiety peaks around 18-24 months and is characterized by rapid calming and engagement in activities once the primary caregiver is out of sight.

questions.retrievalTag: Homeless alcoholic oxalate crystals ethylene glycol
questions.reviewPearl: Ethylene glycol metabolism produces oxalic acid, which binds with calcium to precipitate as calcium oxalate crystals in the renal tubules, leading to acute kidney injury.
questions.stem: A 52-year-old homeless man with alcoholism is brought to the emergency department by police after being found agitated and confused. He says he feels nervous and could use a drink. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 138/86 mm Hg. He is trembling. His hematocrit is 30%. Urinalysis shows 7 WBC/hpf and many oxalate crystals. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify ethylene glycol toxicity based on central nervous system changes and the classic finding of calcium oxalate crystals in the urine.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The presence of calcium oxalate crystals in the urine of an agitated, trembling patient with alcoholism is highly pathognomonic for ethylene glycol ingestion. Homeless individuals with alcohol use disorder may consume antifreeze (ethylene glycol) as a substitute for alcohol.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Methanol toxicity (C) presents similarly with acidosis and altered mental status but typically causes visual disturbances (e.g., "snowfield" vision) and does not produce urinary oxalate crystals.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Ethylene glycol metabolism produces oxalic acid, which binds with calcium to precipitate as calcium oxalate crystals in the renal tubules, leading to acute kidney injury.

questions.retrievalTag: Hand flapping twirling avoids eye contact neologism PDD
questions.reviewPearl: Stereotypic motor movements and lack of joint attention (e.g., poor eye contact) are classic, highly recognizable signs of Autism Spectrum Disorder in young children.
questions.stem: A 5-year-old girl is brought to the physician for a well-child examination. Her mother is concerned because she uses nonsensical and repetitive words. Her teacher reports that other children do not like to play with her, and they laugh at her because she often flaps her hands when she speaks. She frequently spends time alone in her room twirling on her toes. Her mother drank two beers daily and used cocaine occasionally during the pregnancy. The patient is at the 60th percentile for height and 55th percentile for weight. Her pulse is 76/min, and blood pressure is 100/80 mm Hg. Physical examination show eczema and bruising over the knees. Mental status examinations shows a neutral mood. She avoids eye contact and refers to the physician as a "minzer." Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize the triad of impaired social communication, restricted/repetitive behaviors (hand-flapping), and delayed language/neologisms as hallmarks of Autism Spectrum Disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient's presentation of neologisms ("minzer"), poor eye contact, impaired social interaction with peers, and stereotypic motor behaviors (hand flapping, twirling) strongly indicate a Pervasive Developmental Disorder (Autism Spectrum Disorder).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: While her mother drank alcohol during pregnancy, Fetal alcohol syndrome (B) typically presents with distinct facial dysmorphisms, microcephaly, and growth restriction (she is at the 60th/55th percentiles). ADHD (A) does not explain the neologisms or stereotypic motor behaviors.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Stereotypic motor movements and lack of joint attention (e.g., poor eye contact) are classic, highly recognizable signs of Autism Spectrum Disorder in young children.

questions.retrievalTag: Bipolar ibuprofen exercise tremor nystagmus lithium toxicity
questions.reviewPearl: NSAIDs reduce renal blood flow and dramatically decrease lithium clearance, quickly leading to severe neurological toxicity in bipolar patients.
questions.stem: A 47-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her husband because of increasing confusion during the past 2 days. On arrival, she has a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 4 minutes. She has bipolar disorder treated with several medications, but her husband is unsure of their names. He knows that she sometimes takes ibuprofen for mild arthritis pain caused by exercise. He says that she has been active and exercising more lately, but he cannot think of other changes in her routine. She has no history of seizure disorder. She is oriented to person but not to place and time. Her pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg. On physical examination, she is tremulous and somnolent. There is bilateral nystagmus. An ECG shows a second-degree atrioventricular block. The most likely cause of this patient’s symptoms is an adverse effect of which of the following medications?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify medications (NSAIDs, thiazides, ACE inhibitors) and physical states (volume depletion) that decrease renal lithium clearance and precipitate toxicity.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has a history of bipolar disorder and presents with classic signs of lithium toxicity: confusion, seizures, tremor, and bilateral nystagmus. Her recent heavy exercise (leading to volume depletion) and use of ibuprofen (an NSAID) significantly impaired her renal lithium clearance, precipitating the toxic state.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: While bupropion (A) lowers the seizure threshold, it does not typically cause this constellation of severe tremor, nystagmus, and AV block. Valproic acid (E) toxicity typically causes hepatotoxicity or hyperammonemic encephalopathy, not this classical presentation.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: NSAIDs reduce renal blood flow and dramatically decrease lithium clearance, quickly leading to severe neurological toxicity in bipolar patients.

questions.retrievalTag: Clozapine flu-like symptoms low leukocyte count discontinue
questions.reviewPearl: Clozapine therapy mandates strict, protocolized monitoring of absolute neutrophil counts (ANC); if a patient develops fever or signs of infection, a stat CBC must be drawn and the drug immediately stopped if severe neutropenia is present.
questions.stem: A 28-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of flu-like symptoms and cough productive of clear sputum for 24 hours. He has been hospitalized eight times over the past 5 years because of bizarre delusions and hallucinations during which a voice tells him what to do. He has a 9-year history of type 1 diabetes mellitus. In addition to insulin, he is receiving clozapine therapy (400 mg daily) for his psychotic condition. During mental status examination, he is hallucinating and says that he is being pursued by special agents of the government. Leukocyte count is 2750/mm3, and serum glucose concentration is 140 mg/d

answerChoices.label: L
answerChoices.text: Urinalysis is within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

questions.educationalObjective: Recognize clozapine-induced agranulocytosis and understand that immediate discontinuation of the medication is required.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is on clozapine and presents with flu-like symptoms and a dangerously low leukocyte count (2750/mm3) indicating clozapine-induced neutropenia/agranulocytosis. The immediate and most critical next step is the total discontinuation of clozapine.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Decreasing the dose (C) is insufficient; life-threatening agranulocytosis requires absolute cessation of the offending agent. Increasing the dose (E) would worsen the bone marrow suppression.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Clozapine therapy mandates strict, protocolized monitoring of absolute neutrophil counts (ANC); if a patient develops fever or signs of infection, a stat CBC must be drawn and the drug immediately stopped if severe neutropenia is present.

questions.retrievalTag: 7-year-old stomachaches quit carpool separation anxiety
questions.reviewPearl: School refusal in young children, accompanied by morning somatic complaints that resolve if the child is allowed to stay home, is a classic presentation of Separation Anxiety Disorder.
questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the physician by his mother because of difficulty at school since beginning first grade 6 weeks ago. His teachers state that he has been daydreaming frequently, does not complete his work, and does not participate in classroom discussions. His mother reports that he is very shy, will not go to sleepovers, and has stomachaches every weekday morning. He played on a soccer team, but he quit when his mother could not drive him to games and he had to carpool with another family. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, the boy sits on his mother’s lap, does not make eye contact with the physician, and is hesitant to answer questions but seems eager to please. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify Separation Anxiety Disorder by recognizing somatic symptoms and avoidance behaviors triggered specifically by separation from a major attachment figure.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The child displays excessive distress upon separation from his mother, manifested by physical symptoms (stomachaches), avoidance of activities without his mother (quitting soccer, avoiding sleepovers), and clinging behavior. This is characteristic of Separation Anxiety Disorder, which often presents with school refusal.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: While he daydreams, ADHD (A) does not explain the somatic symptoms or the specific refusal to carpool without his mother. Social phobia (E) involves fear of scrutiny, whereas this child's anxiety is linked purely to maternal separation.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: School refusal in young children, accompanied by morning somatic complaints that resolve if the child is allowed to stay home, is a classic presentation of Separation Anxiety Disorder.

questions.retrievalTag: Fluoxetine tranylcypromine 1 week Serotonin Syndrome
questions.reviewPearl: Always wait at least 5 weeks after discontinuing fluoxetine before initiating an MAOI due to fluoxetine's exceptionally long half-life.
questions.stem: A 27-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after his brother found him agitated, tremulous, and complaining of a headache. He had eaten a peanut butter sandwich 12 hours before his symptoms began. He has a 10-year history of severe major depressive disorder. During this period, he has attempted suicide three times despite treatment with both selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and tricyclic antidepressants. Eight days ago, he went to a new physician because of increasing despondency. This physician immediately discontinued the fluoxetine and substituted tranylcypromine to start 1 week later. He also instructed the patient to start a monoamine oxidase inhibitor diet immediately. Current medications also include diphenhydramine as needed for seasonal allergies. He has no history of hypertension, diabetes mellitus, or migraine. On arrival, he is agitated. His temperature is 40.6°C (105°F), pulse is 130/min, respirations are 26/min, and blood pressure is 180/120 mm Hg. Physical examination shows diaphoresis and tremor of the upper extremities. Deep tendon reflexes are 3+ bilaterally; there are no pathologic reflexes. On mental status examination, he is complaining of occipital headache. He is oriented to person but not to place or time. Which of the following is the most likely cause of these findings?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize Serotonin Syndrome resulting from an inadequate washout period between an SSRI with a long half-life (fluoxetine) and an MAOI.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has Serotonin Syndrome caused by a drug-drug interaction between fluoxetine and the MAOI tranylcypromine. Fluoxetine has a very long half-life, and at least 5 weeks must elapse before starting an MAOI to prevent this life-threatening interaction.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Ingestion of tyramine-rich foods causes a hypertensive crisis, but peanut butter (D) is not high in tyramine. Fluoxetine withdrawal (B) does not cause severe autonomic instability and hyperreflexia.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Always wait at least 5 weeks after discontinuing fluoxetine before initiating an MAOI due to fluoxetine's exceptionally long half-life.

questions.retrievalTag: Alzheimer amitriptyline visual hallucinations tiny man
questions.reviewPearl: Avoid TCAs like amitriptyline in elderly patients, especially those with pre-existing dementia, due to their profound anticholinergic side effects and risk of precipitating delirium.
questions.stem: An 82-year-old man with dementia, Alzheimer type, is brought to the physician because of a 4-day history of visual hallucinations and irritability. He has osteoarthritis, hypertension, and hypothyroidism. His medications are rivastigmine, ibuprofen, atenolol, levothyroxine, and amitriptyline. His blood pressure is 128/88 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he recalls zero of the three objects after 5 minutes. During the examination, he points his finger and sternly says, "You need to stop doing that right this minute." When asked whom he is addressing, he says, "that tiny little man shouting over in the corner." Results of a complete blood count, measurement of serum glucose concentration, and kidney and liver function tests are within the reference ranges. Urinalysis shows no abnormalities. Discontinuation of which of the following of this patient's medications is most appropriate at this time?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify amitriptyline and other highly anticholinergic medications as common iatrogenic causes of delirium in the elderly.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Amitriptyline is a tricyclic antidepressant with potent anticholinergic properties. Elderly patients with dementia are highly sensitive to anticholinergic medications, which frequently precipitate delirium and visual hallucinations.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Rivastigmine (E) is a cholinesterase inhibitor used to treat Alzheimer's and would not cause anticholinergic delirium. Ibuprofen, atenolol, and levothyroxine (B, C, D) are much less likely to cause acute psychiatric changes.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Avoid TCAs like amitriptyline in elderly patients, especially those with pre-existing dementia, due to their profound anticholinergic side effects and risk of precipitating delirium.

questions.retrievalTag: 2-year-old isolated slap no impact
questions.reviewPearl: Though corporal punishment is discouraged, isolated and minor instances without a pattern of ongoing abuse do not typically derail normal psychological development or cause PTSD.
questions.stem: In a fit of anger, a 22-year-old woman slaps her 2-year-old child across the face. There is no other history of abuse. Which of the following is the most likely outcome of this experience for the child?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize that an isolated incident of minor physical trauma without chronic abuse is unlikely to cause a permanent psychiatric disorder in a child.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: While the mother's action is inappropriate and concerning, a single, isolated incident of physical discipline/slapping without a chronic pattern of abuse or severe trauma is unlikely to result in a long-term psychiatric disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: PTSD (D) requires exposure to actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence. Brief psychotic (A), dysthymic (B), and pain disorders (C) are not directly caused by a single isolated slap.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Though corporal punishment is discouraged, isolated and minor instances without a pattern of ongoing abuse do not typically derail normal psychological development or cause PTSD.

questions.retrievalTag: Vivid dream physical threat wakes up nightmare disorder
questions.reviewPearl: Nightmares occur during REM sleep and are remembered vividly, whereas sleep terrors occur during non-REM sleep and leave the patient with amnesia of the event.
questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman comes to student health services because of a 6-week history of difficulty sleeping. She says that two to three times weekly, she wakes up during the night in the middle of a vivid dream that usually involves the need to escape from a physical threat such as a rapist. When she tries to run in her dreams, her legs feel progressively heavier until she is barely able to move; it is at this point that she wakes up. She says that she has difficulty falling back to sleep; during the past 2 weeks, she has been drinking a glass of wine to aid her in falling back to sleep. She feels tired throughout the day and is concerned that her grades will begin to decline. Three months ago, she broke up with her boyfriend of 1 year because he refused to stop using marijuana. She has not dated since the breakup, but she spends time with friends. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she is friendly and cooperative. She appears mildly anxious, but she smiles and laughs on occasion. She says she is tired. Her thought process is logical and goal directed. She is oriented to person, place, and time. There is no evidence of auditory or visual hallucinations. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Differentiate nightmare disorder from sleep terror disorder based on detailed dream recall and full wakefulness upon arousal.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient's primary issue is recurrent, highly detailed, frightening dreams (nightmares) that awaken her, followed by difficulty returning to sleep and subsequent daytime fatigue. This meets the criteria for Nightmare disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Sleep terror disorder (F) involves abrupt incomplete awakenings (often with a panicky scream), severe autonomic arousal, and importantly, lack of detailed dream recall. Her distress centers directly on the dreams, making Primary insomnia (E) incorrect.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Nightmares occur during REM sleep and are remembered vividly, whereas sleep terrors occur during non-REM sleep and leave the patient with amnesia of the event.

questions.retrievalTag: Forgetful pot unattended stove next step MMSE
questions.reviewPearl: Always objectively quantify cognitive deficits with standardized tools like the MMSE or MoCA before pursuing expensive imaging or neurocognitive medications.
questions.stem: An 82-year-old man is brought to the physician by his daughter because he has been forgetful during the past 6 weeks. She is particularly concerned because a small fire started recently when he left a pot unattended on the stove. The patient's wife died 1 year ago, and he says he has been distracted recently and cannot stop thinking about her. He lives alone and pays his bills, does routine household chores, and drives short distances for errands. His daughter says he was doing well on his own until recently. He is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 63 kg (140 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 78/min and regular, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is alert and cooperative. He becomes very sad when he talks about his wife's death. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: Utilize the MMSE as the initial objective assessment tool for elderly patients presenting with new-onset memory impairment.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: In an elderly patient presenting with acute or subacute cognitive decline and functional impairment (leaving a pot on the stove), an objective bedside cognitive assessment, such as the Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE), is the essential first step before ordering imaging or starting medications.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Recommending assisted living (C) or starting donepezil (E) is premature before characterizing the deficit. A CT scan (D) might be indicated later to rule out reversible causes, but only after initial cognitive screening. Delaying evaluation by 3 months (A) is unsafe due to the fire risk.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Always objectively quantify cognitive deficits with standardized tools like the MMSE or MoCA before pursuing expensive imaging or neurocognitive medications.

questions.retrievalTag: 6-year-old no friends eye contact drawings haystacks Asperger
questions.reviewPearl: Children with Asperger syndrome typically have preserved cognitive and language development but show severe deficits in nonverbal social communication and exhibit highly restricted, repetitive interests.
questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of difficulty in school since he began first grade 2 months ago. He tells his parents that he hates school and does not want to go. He did not attend preschool. His first-grade teacher describes him as a daydreamer who does not pay attention and will not read or write. She reports that he has no friends and does not listen to her. She admits that she is getting quite frustrated with him because he will not look at her when she speaks to him. She states that he is good with numbers and prefers math to reading and spelling. She has noticed that he fills his notebook with drawings of haystacks that look identical. She says that he does not seem to be happy in school. Recent psychoeducational testing showed a performance IQ of 90 and a verbal IQ of 90. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows no evidence of psychosis, mania, or depression. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize Asperger disorder (Autism Spectrum Disorder without language or cognitive impairment) by identifying social deficits, poor eye contact, and intensely restricted interests in the setting of normal intelligence.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: This child has impaired social interactions (no friends, poor eye contact), restricted/repetitive behaviors (drawing identical haystacks), and normal intelligence (IQ 90), which classically points to Asperger disorder (now classified under Autism Spectrum Disorder).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Mild mental retardation (C) is ruled out by his normal IQ scores. Reading disorder (D) does not account for his profound social deficits and fixations. Separation anxiety (E) causes school refusal but does not explain poor eye contact and repetitive behaviors.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Children with Asperger syndrome typically have preserved cognitive and language development but show severe deficits in nonverbal social communication and exhibit highly restricted, repetitive interests.

questions.retrievalTag: Loss of libido guilty husband died hypoactive sexual desire
questions.reviewPearl: Psychological factors, such as unresolved grief or guilt, are frequent primary drivers of Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder, even in post-menopausal women on hormone replacement therapy.
questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman comes to the physician because of loss of libido for 9 months. She was sexually active with her husband until he died 3 years ago. Since meeting her 58-year-old male friend 3 months ago, she has had consistent excuses for not engaging in sexual intercourse. She feels guilty about starting a new relationship so soon after her husband's death and has had recurrent dreams over the past 6 months about her husband and their happy marriage. She has not had crying spells or difficulty with sleep or appetite. She has been receiving hormone replacement therapy since undergoing a total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy 5 years ago. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder in patients with a persistent deficit in sexual desire causing marked distress or interpersonal difficulty.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient lacks sexual desire/libido despite an opportunity for a relationship, causing significant personal distress/avoidance, which fulfills the criteria for Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder. Her symptoms are heavily influenced by psychological factors (guilt over her deceased husband).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Normal aging (E) shouldn't completely eradicate libido or cause distress and active avoidance. Sexual arousal disorder (B) involves the inability to attain physiological arousal (e.g., lubrication), whereas this is a primary lack of desire. Vaginismus (D) is painful involuntary muscle spasm during penetration.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Psychological factors, such as unresolved grief or guilt, are frequent primary drivers of Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder, even in post-menopausal women on hormone replacement therapy.

questions.retrievalTag: Synthetic heroin stiffness drooling substantia nigra MPTP
questions.reviewPearl: MPTP toxicity provides a human model for Parkinson's disease because it irreversibly and acutely destroys the dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta.
questions.stem: An 18-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by friends 1 hour after they found him on the couch at a party unable to move. Earlier that evening, he had been using synthetic heroin. On arrival, his pulse is 85/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Examination shows stiffness in all extremities, drooling, and slow response to questions. Which of the following brain regions is most likely affected?
questions.educationalObjective: Associate synthetic heroin (MPTP) use with the rapid onset of parkinsonian symptoms due to the destruction of the substantia nigra.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient exhibits sudden, severe parkinsonian symptoms (rigidity, bradykinesia, drooling) after using synthetic heroin. This is pathognomonic for MPTP toxicity, a byproduct of synthetic heroin manufacturing that selectively destroys dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: The locus ceruleus (C) produces norepinephrine. Mammillary bodies (D) are affected in Wernicke encephalopathy (thiamine deficiency). The cerebellum (A) controls coordination, not extrapyramidal rigidity.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: MPTP toxicity provides a human model for Parkinson's disease because it irreversibly and acutely destroys the dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta.

questions.retrievalTag: Chronic pain tired of all this like this forever giving up
questions.reviewPearl: Always directly inquire about suicidal thoughts when a patient expresses profound hopelessness or despair, particularly in high-risk populations like those suffering from debilitating chronic pain.
questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman comes to the physician because of increasingly severe pain that originates in her left shoulder and radiates to her elbow. She describes the pain as constant and burning, rating her current pain as a 7 on a 10-point scale. Eighteen months ago, she sustained a nerve injury of the left upper extremity in a motor vehicle collision. Since that time, she has been unable to return to work. Muscle strength in the left forearm and finger flexors is 3/5. On sensory examination, there is severe pain with light stroking of the anterolateral aspect of the left arm. Further sensory testing is deferred. During the examination she tells her physician, "I'm tired of all this. My medication is not strong enough. It only takes the edge off my pain, which is only getting worse. I'm realizing I'll be like this forever." Which of the following is the most appropriate response by the physician?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: "Are you worried about more nerve damage developing?"

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: "Do you ever use more pain medication than is prescribed?"

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: "Have you been feeling like just giving up?"

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: "Is the pain caused by touch socially limiting?"

questions.educationalObjective: Recognize statements of hopelessness in chronic pain patients as red flags requiring immediate assessment for depression and suicidal ideation.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient's statements ("I'm tired of all this," "I'll be like this forever") express profound hopelessness, a key indicator of severe depression. Patients with chronic pain syndromes are at a significantly elevated risk for suicide. The physician must directly screen for suicidal ideation.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Options A, B, and D explore specific symptom details but ignore the immediate psychological distress and safety risk. Option E is dismissive of her emotional statement and fails to address the underlying despair.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Always directly inquire about suicidal thoughts when a patient expresses profound hopelessness or despair, particularly in high-risk populations like those suffering from debilitating chronic pain.

questions.retrievalTag: 8-year-old stomachaches worry band soccer GAD
questions.reviewPearl: In children, GAD frequently presents as a "perfectionist" profile with excessive worry about competence and performance, often leading to somatic complaints like stomachaches.
questions.stem: An 8-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her mother because of frequent stomachaches over the past 3 months. She has a 1-year history of difficulty falling asleep and poor concentration. She has always been a good student and excels in extracurricular activities. Her parents divorced 6 months ago. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows an anxious affect and coherent thought process. She says that she is worried about her classes and about her performance in the school band and on the soccer team. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose Generalized Anxiety Disorder in a pediatric patient presenting with excessive worry across multiple performance areas accompanied by somatic symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has excessive, out-of-proportion anxiety regarding multiple domains (school, band, soccer) accompanied by somatic symptoms (stomachaches, insomnia, poor concentration) lasting over 6 months, which characterizes Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Her anxiety is focused on performance, not separation from her parents (E). There is no mention of obsessions/compulsions (B), panic attacks (C), or a qualifying trauma like abuse or violence (D; divorce does not qualify).

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: In children, GAD frequently presents as a "perfectionist" profile with excessive worry about competence and performance, often leading to somatic complaints like stomachaches.

questions.retrievalTag: Anxiety major speech family history alcohol propranolol
questions.reviewPearl: Beta-blockers are the treatment of choice for acute performance anxiety to suppress autonomic symptoms (tremor, tachycardia) without causing cognitive impairment.
questions.stem: For this patient with anxiety, select the most appropriate pharmacotherapy.

answerChoices.label: I
answerChoices.text:
Propranolol
A 27-year-old man comes to the physician because of anxiety about a major speech that he must deliver in 3 days. He is excited about the opportunity for his career but is concerned that his apprehension and great fear of public speaking will affect his performance. He requests a tranquilizer to help with his anxiety. He has no history of medical problems. He has a family history of alcohol dependence. He does not drink alcohol or use drugs. His pulse is 76/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 130/82 mm Hg. Examination shows normal findings.

questions.educationalObjective: Select propranolol as the first-line pharmacotherapy for isolated performance anxiety in an otherwise healthy patient.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has performance-only social anxiety disorder (fear of public speaking). Given his lack of medical contraindications (no asthma or cardiac issues) and his family history of alcohol dependence (making benzodiazepines a riskier choice), a beta-blocker like propranolol is the most appropriate treatment to control autonomic symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Lorazepam (G) is a benzodiazepine that carries a risk of dependence, especially given his family history, and can cause cognitive blunting during his speech. Fluoxetine (D) and Buspirone (B) require weeks to take effect, making them useless for an event in 3 days.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Beta-blockers are the treatment of choice for acute performance anxiety to suppress autonomic symptoms (tremor, tachycardia) without causing cognitive impairment.

questions.retrievalTag: Anxiety major speech asthma theophylline lorazepam
questions.reviewPearl: Always check for a history of asthma or reactive airway disease before prescribing non-selective beta-blockers for performance anxiety.
questions.stem: For this patient with anxiety, select the most appropriate pharmacotherapy.

answerChoices.label: I
answerChoices.text:
Propranolol
A 27-year-old man comes to the physician because of anxiety about a major speech that he must deliver in 3 days. He has a great fear of public speaking and is convinced that his apprehension and tremulous delivery will damage his performance. He requests a tranquilizer to help with his anxiety. He takes theophylline and uses corticosteroid and albuterol inhalers for asthma. He has a family history of alcohol dependence. He does not drink alcohol or use drugs. His pulse is 66/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 132/80 mm Hg. Examination shows normal findings.

questions.educationalObjective: Recognize that beta-blockers are contraindicated for performance anxiety in patients with reactive airway disease, necessitating cautious use of an alternative like a short-acting benzodiazepine.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: While propranolol is normally the drug of choice for performance anxiety, it is strictly contraindicated in this patient due to his active asthma (he takes theophylline and albuterol). Therefore, a short course of a benzodiazepine like lorazepam is the best available option for rapid anxiolysis, despite his family history of alcohol dependence.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Propranolol (I) can trigger fatal bronchospasm in asthmatic patients. Buspirone (B) and Fluoxetine (D) take several weeks to work, making them ineffective for an event 3 days away.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Always check for a history of asthma or reactive airway disease before prescribing non-selective beta-blockers for performance anxiety.

questions.retrievalTag: Inventions new RV God talking to me Bipolar
questions.reviewPearl: Co-occurring substance use (like cannabis) is extremely common during manic episodes, but a primary diagnosis of Bipolar I should be made if the patient has a long-standing history of episodic mood disturbances.
questions.stem: A 47-year-old man is brought to the physician by his wife because of “unbearable” behavior during the past 2 weeks. His wife reports that he has been talking about his various inventions constantly and recently bought a new recreational vehicle, even though they do not travel long distances. He repeatedly tells his wife that “the time to make and enjoy money has come.” He has been openly critical of the pastor’s prayers during church services. He claims that the pastor does not know what he is talking about because “God is talking to me, not him.” His wife says that he has had episodes of similar behavior during the past 10 years that have been more tolerable. He has peptic ulcer disease treated with ranitidine and hypertension treated with triamterene. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. He is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 82 kg (180 lb); BMI is 28 kg/m2. His pulse is 80/min, and blood pressure is 150/95 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no other abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is relaxed and talkative, jumping from one topic to another. He claims that his wife does not understand him and that she has been plotting with the pastor against him. He denies any problems and says that he feels great and is capable of great things. Laboratory findings are within the reference range. Urine toxicology screening is positive for marijuana. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Mood disorder due to a general medical condition

questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose Bipolar I disorder in a patient with a long-term history of episodic manic symptoms, recognizing that a positive drug screen does not override a primary psychiatric diagnosis when the longitudinal history is classic.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient presents with a classic manic episode featuring grandiosity ("God is talking to me"), impulsivity (buying an RV), flight of ideas, and elevated mood. His 10-year history of similar episodic behavior confirms Bipolar I disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Marijuana use is a common comorbidity, but cannabis does not typically cause a full-blown manic episode lasting 2 weeks, making a substance-induced mood disorder (E) incorrect. Delusional disorder (B) does not encompass the expansive mood, flight of ideas, or impulsive financial behavior.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Co-occurring substance use (like cannabis) is extremely common during manic episodes, but a primary diagnosis of Bipolar I should be made if the patient has a long-standing history of episodic mood disturbances.

questions.retrievalTag: Aphonia cholecystectomy appendectomy hysterectomy somatization
questions.reviewPearl: A patient under 30 presenting with a "thick chart" of negative diagnostic tests and multiple abdominal surgeries for chronic pain should immediately raise suspicion for a somatic symptom disorder.
questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman is hospitalized for evaluation of a 5-month history of headaches. While recovering from sedation with lorazepam for cerebral angiography, she has dull, aching abdominal pain that is localized to the right upper quadrant of the abdomen without radiation. It has occurred several times over the past week after meals. She has had no nausea or vomiting. Following a CT scan 3 months ago, she had aphonia for 48 hours. She has a history of cholecystectomy, appendectomy, hysterectomy, and exploratory laparoscopy for endometriosis. Cerebral angiography shows normal findings. Repeat neurologic examinations and CT scan of the head are negative. Her temperature is 36.4ºC (97.6ºF), pulse is 68/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 122/76 mm Hg. During examination, she complains of unbearable pain, although the abdomen is tender to light palpation without guarding or rebound. No masses are palpated. Bowel sounds are normal. Pelvic examination shows normal findings. Test of the stool for occult blood is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify Somatization disorder based on a long history of seeking treatment for multiple unexplained somatic complaints resulting in numerous negative invasive procedures and surgeries.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has a profound history of multiple unexplained physical symptoms (headaches, abdominal pain, pseudoneurological aphonia) that have resulted in extensive, negative medical workups and multiple surgical organ removals (cholecystectomy, appendectomy, hysterectomy). This chronic, multi-organ pattern is the hallmark of Somatization Disorder (currently classified as Somatic Symptom Disorder).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Conversion disorder (C) applies strictly to isolated neurological symptoms (like the aphonia), not the extensive multi-system pain and gastrointestinal complaints. Acute intermittent porphyria (A) can cause abdominal pain and neurological symptoms but would not typically explain decades of purely functional pain leading to multiple organ removals without other metabolic signs.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
