body: A patient under 30 presenting with a "thick chart" of negative diagnostic tests and multiple abdominal surgeries for chronic pain should immediately raise suspicion for a somatic symptom disorder.

questions.retrievalTag: Haloperidol rigid temperature 103 elevated CK drug reaction
questions.reviewPearl: Elevated creatine kinase (CK) and leukocytosis are critical laboratory markers that help differentiate NMS from a primary psychiatric catatonic state.
questions.stem: A 24-year-old man is brought to the emergency department from a psychiatric hospital because of a decreasing level of consciousness over the past 3 days. He has become progressively less responsive and has remained in bed for the past 12 hours without speaking or following commands. He has a history of schizophrenia with multiple psychiatric hospitalizations and is currently being treated with haloperidol and benztropine. His temperature is 39.4oC (103oF), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 28/min, and blood pressure is 180/100 mm Hg. He withdraws weakly to noxious stimuli. His eyes are open, but he does not fix or follow visual stimuli or gaze around the room. He is mute and does not follow commands. Examination shows marked rigidity of the neck and all extremities. Laboratory studies show: Leukocyte count 18,000/mm3 Segmented neutrophils 80% Lymphocytes 20% Serum creatine kinase 12,000 U/L Cerebrospinal fluid Erythrocyte count 2/mm3, Leukocyte count 1/mm3, Protein 28 mg/dL, Glucose 78 mg/d

answerChoices.label: L
answerChoices.text: A CT scan of the head shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause?

questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS) as an adverse drug reaction in a patient taking an antipsychotic who presents with hyperthermia, extreme rigidity, autonomic instability, and elevated CK.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: This patient is experiencing a life-threatening drug reaction: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS) secondary to haloperidol. The clinical tetrad of NMS includes altered mental status (mutism), autonomic instability (tachycardia, severe hypertension), hyperthermia (103ºF), and severe "lead-pipe" rigidity, accompanied by dramatically elevated creatine kinase (12,000 U/L) from muscle breakdown.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Catatonia (A) can cause rigidity and mutism but lacks the extreme hyperthermia, severe autonomic instability, and massive CK elevation. An infection like meningitis/encephalitis (E) is ruled out by the normal CSF profile.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Elevated creatine kinase (CK) and leukocytosis are critical laboratory markers that help differentiate NMS from a primary psychiatric catatonic state.

questions.retrievalTag: Famous gospel singer calm affect hears voice Lord schizoaffective
questions.reviewPearl: If a patient has a history of severe mood episodes but continues to hallucinate when their mood is completely stable, the diagnosis is Schizoaffective Disorder.
questions.stem: Five weeks after being discharged from the hospital after treatment for a psychotic episode, a 27-year-old man comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. During hospitalization, he claimed that he was instructed by the Lord to find a new religion and that a famous gospel singer was in love with him. Today, he says that he still hears the voice of the Lord and members of the church he attends in his apartment and when he shops in the supermarket. He no longer believes the world needs a new religion because the Lord is no longer instructing him to create one. He states, “My father in heaven tells me that he is at peace, and therefore, so am I.” Current medications include risperidone and lorazepam. He drinks one to two beers on weekends. He used marijuana regularly in college but has abstained for the past 5 years. He appears clean and is casually dressed. His temperature is 36.7oC (98oF), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 130/72 mm Hg. Physical examination shows a calm affect. He is cooperative, alert, and oriented to person, place, and time. Based on this information, which of the following is the most likely current diagnosis for this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: Differentiate Schizoaffective Disorder from Bipolar Disorder with psychotic features by identifying the persistence of psychotic symptoms for at least 2 weeks in the absence of a major mood episode.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient's initial hospitalization involved a manic episode with psychotic features (grandiose/erotomanic delusions). Currently, he is completely euthymic ("calm affect", "at peace") but continues to experience prominent psychotic symptoms (auditory hallucinations) 5 weeks later. Psychosis occurring for more than 2 weeks in the absence of a major mood episode is the defining criterion for Schizoaffective Disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body:
In Bipolar disorder with psychotic features (A), the psychotic symptoms must only occur
during
the manic or depressive mood episodes; they do not persist when the mood is euthymic. Delusional disorder (C) lacks prominent auditory hallucinations and manic episodes.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: If a patient has a history of severe mood episodes but continues to hallucinate when their mood is completely stable, the diagnosis is Schizoaffective Disorder.

questions.retrievalTag: Dilated pupils long breaks loquacious lethargic cocaine
questions.reviewPearl: Unexplained long absences during the workday followed by extreme energetic behavior and paranoia should highly raise suspicion for short-acting stimulant abuse, such as cocaine.
questions.stem: A 32-year-old financial analyst comes to the physician at her employer’s request because of bizarre behavior and poor work productivity over the past 6 months. She has been arriving late, leaving early, and frequently taking long breaks. She has had daily mood swings; sometimes she appears withdrawn, lethargic, and belligerent and other times she appears energetic and loquacious. This morning, she threatened to physically assault her supervisor after he questioned her about a 2-hour absence from work. Her temperature is 37oC (98.6oF), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 160/100 mm Hg. The pupils are dilated and reactive to light. Deep tendon reflexes are increased. On mental status examination, she is angry but cooperative. She describes her supervisor’s unfairness and the unfriendliness of other analysts; she suspects that they are jealous of her. She speaks in a loud rapid voice, frequently interrupting the physician. She is oriented to person, place, and time. She is able to recall three of three objects after 5 minutes and accurately preforms 2-digit calculations. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify cocaine abuse based on behavioral signs (frequent breaks, rapid mood swings) and physical findings of sympathetic overactivity (dilated pupils, hypertension, rapid speech).

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient's frequent long breaks from work, alternating between energetic/loquacious states and lethargic crashes, coupled with dilated pupils, hypertension, hyperreflexia, and paranoid ideation are highly characteristic of cocaine intoxication and withdrawal.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Bipolar disorder (B) does not typically cause acutely dilated pupils or daily, short-acting cycles of mania and lethargy tied to work breaks. PCP abuse (G) typically presents with prominent nystagmus, severe aggression, and dissociation. Paranoid personality disorder (F) does not involve pupillary dilation or extreme swings in energy levels.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Unexplained long absences during the workday followed by extreme energetic behavior and paranoia should highly raise suspicion for short-acting stimulant abuse, such as cocaine.

questions.retrievalTag: Ballet practice BMI 16 tibial stress fracture anorexia
questions.reviewPearl: "Euthyroid sick syndrome" (characterized by low T3 levels with normal TSH) and early-onset severe osteoporosis are frequent secondary complications of the starvation state in Anorexia Nervosa.
questions.stem: A 15-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department 90 minutes after falling during ballet practice. She says that she has recently felt weak but has not had fever or other symptoms. She reports that she has felt “down” and “disgusted” with how she looks for the past 5 months. During this time, she has had difficulty sleeping and a decreased appetite resulting in a 6.8-kg (15-lb) weight loss. Menarche has not yet occurred. She is not sexually active and does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. She appears younger than her stated age. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 42 kg (92 lb); BMI is 16 kg/m2. Physical examination shows tenderness to palpation over the right tibia. On mental status examination, she makes poor eye contact, speaks softly, and answers questions with vague responses. Her leukocyte count is 2800/mm3, and serum triiodothyronine (T3) concentration is 95 ng/d

answerChoices.label: L
answerChoices.text: An x-ray of the right lower extremity shows a tibial stress fracture and significant osteoporosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose Anorexia Nervosa by recognizing significantly low body weight, body image distortion, and secondary physical manifestations of starvation (osteoporosis, leukopenia, low T3).

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has a significantly low BMI (16), primary amenorrhea, and body image distortion ("disgusted" with how she looks). The resulting malnutrition has led to severe complications including euthyroid sick syndrome (low T3), leukopenia, and osteoporosis resulting in a tibial stress fracture, all classic for severe Anorexia Nervosa.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Major depressive disorder (E) can cause weight loss but does not typically cause body image distortion or such severe primary amenorrhea and osteoporosis in a 15-year-old. Body dysmorphic disorder (B) involves preoccupation with a specific perceived defect, not global weight loss leading to starvation.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: "Euthyroid sick syndrome" (characterized by low T3 levels with normal TSH) and early-onset severe osteoporosis are frequent secondary complications of the starvation state in Anorexia Nervosa.

questions.retrievalTag: Husband died 18 months brain cancer punished by God MDD
questions.reviewPearl: Psychotic features that emerge during severe major depressive episodes are almost always mood-congruent, typically taking the form of somatic delusions (terminal illness) or delusions of guilt, poverty, or deserved punishment.
questions.stem: A 67-year-old woman, whose husband died 18 months ago, is admitted to the hospital because of severe headaches for 4 weeks. She is quiet and seems sad. She believes that she has brain cancer, deserves to be punished for her sins, and is being punished by God for past misdeeds. She has had fatigue and a 6.8-kg (15-lb) weight loss over the past 4 months. Physical and neurologic examinations shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Adjustment disorder with mixed disturbance of emotions and conduct

questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose Major Depressive Disorder with psychotic features when a patient presents with a severe depressive syndrome accompanied by mood-congruent delusions.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient exhibits severe depressive symptoms (sadness, fatigue, significant weight loss) accompanied by mood-congruent psychotic delusions (fixed false beliefs that she has cancer and is being punished by God). This fully characterizes Major Depressive Disorder with psychotic features.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Delusional disorder (B) does not present with pervasive, severe systemic depressive symptoms like massive weight loss and psychomotor retardation. Adjustment disorder (A) does not encompass psychotic symptoms. Somatization disorder (E) requires multiple somatic complaints over many years, not a fixed psychotic delusion of cancer in the setting of severe depression.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Psychotic features that emerge during severe major depressive episodes are almost always mood-congruent, typically taking the form of somatic delusions (terminal illness) or delusions of guilt, poverty, or deserved punishment.

questions.retrievalTag: 15-year-old raucous unwanted sexual advance unsteady gait alcohol
questions.reviewPearl: Severe ataxia and bold behavioral disinhibition coupled with completely normal pupillary reflexes and vital signs point strongly toward alcohol rather than stimulant or hallucinogen intoxication.
questions.stem: A 15-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his two friends because of unusual behavior that began at a party 1 hour ago. His friends say that he became raucous and made an unwanted sexual advance to a girl he had just met. He then ran into the street, careless to oncoming cars, saying he was able to avoid them. His pulse is 84/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. He is agitated when left alone, but he calms down when spoken to by nursing staff. Physical examination shows an unsteady gait. Pupils are equal and reactive. Which of the following is the most likely drug taken?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize acute alcohol intoxication based on behavioral disinhibition, impaired judgment, and ataxia in the setting of normal vital signs and pupillary reflexes.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Alcohol intoxication classically presents with disinhibition (inappropriate sexual advances, raucous behavior), impaired judgment (running into traffic), and ataxia (unsteady gait), all while preserving normal pupillary responses and stable vital signs.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Amphetamine (B) and Cocaine (C) cause profound sympathetic activation, including tachycardia, hypertension, and pupillary dilation. Heroin (D) causes severe CNS depression, bradycardia, and pinpoint pupils. LSD (E) induces hallucinations and mydriasis. Marijuana (F) features conjunctival injection, tachycardia, and increased appetite rather than this degree of severe ataxia and impulsive disinhibition.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Severe ataxia and bold behavioral disinhibition coupled with completely normal pupillary reflexes and vital signs point strongly toward alcohol rather than stimulant or hallucinogen intoxication.

questions.retrievalTag: Daytime sleepiness vivid dream-like images decreased REM latency narcolepsy
questions.reviewPearl: Narcolepsy is characterized by the abnormal intrusion of REM sleep into wakefulness; consequently, patients often enter REM sleep within 15 minutes of sleep onset (decreased REM latency).
questions.stem: A 26-year-old man comes to the physician because of increasing daytime sleepiness over the past 3 years. He has begun to unexpectedly fall asleep at work and is concerned that he will lose his job if he does not change his behavior. He has stopped driving because of fear that he will fall asleep at the wheel. He generally sleeps well at night. He states that he avoids exercise and caffeine ingestion in the evening and does not read or watch television in bed. His wife states that he does not snore or have restless leg movements while sleeping. He has vivid dream-like images just before falling asleep and on awakening. He describes his mood as generally good despite his worry about his job security. Polysomnography is most likely to show which of the following?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify narcolepsy based on excessive daytime sleepiness paired with sleep-related hallucinations, and associate it with a shortened REM latency on sleep studies.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has classic symptoms of narcolepsy, including excessive daytime sleepiness ("sleep attacks") and hypnagogic/hypnopompic hallucinations (vivid dreams on falling asleep and waking). The physiological hallmark of narcolepsy is early-onset REM sleep, measured on polysomnography as a decreased REM latency.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Sleep efficiency in narcolepsy is typically normal or decreased, not increased (D). Sleep latency is dramatically decreased, not increased (E), because these patients fall asleep almost instantly. Stage 1 sleep and REM density (A, C) are not the primary, defining polysomnographic markers of narcolepsy.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Narcolepsy is characterized by the abnormal intrusion of REM sleep into wakefulness; consequently, patients often enter REM sleep within 15 minutes of sleep onset (decreased REM latency).

questions.retrievalTag: Haloperidol smacks lips protrudes tongue discontinue begin risperidone
questions.reviewPearl: When tardive dyskinesia develops, switching to an atypical antipsychotic (such as clozapine, quetiapine, or risperidone) is the standard initial step because their lower D2 receptor affinity decreases the risk of worsening motor symptoms.
questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman comes to the physician for a routine follow-up examination. She has received inpatient psychiatric treatment several times since the age of 25 years for auditory hallucinations and the belief that her thoughts and movements were being controlled by a local television station; her last admission to the hospital was 10 years ago. Treatment with haloperidol for the past 20 years has decreased the occurrence of symptoms. She attempted suicide at the ages of 30 and 38 years. She lives in a supervised residence and does not work. During the examination, she repeatedly smacks her lips and slightly protrudes her tongue. When the physician asks if she is chewing gum, she laughs and opens her mouth to show she is not. She can hold her mouth and tongue still when asked but begins lip smacking when the physician resumes the examination. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Manage emergent tardive dyskinesia by tapering the offending first-generation antipsychotic and substituting it with a second-generation agent.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has developed tardive dyskinesia (involuntary orofacial movements like lip-smacking and tongue protrusion) as a result of taking haloperidol, a high-potency first-generation antipsychotic, for 20 years. The most appropriate management is to discontinue the offending drug and switch to a second-generation (atypical) antipsychotic, like risperidone, which has a significantly lower risk of causing extrapyramidal symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Continuing haloperidol (A, B) will likely worsen the tardive dyskinesia over time. Completely discontinuing all antipsychotics (C) is dangerous given her severe history of schizophrenia and suicidality. Chlorpromazine (D) is a low-potency first-generation antipsychotic that still carries a high risk for tardive dyskinesia.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: When tardive dyskinesia develops, switching to an atypical antipsychotic (such as clozapine, quetiapine, or risperidone) is the standard initial step because their lower D2 receptor affinity decreases the risk of worsening motor symptoms.

questions.retrievalTag: Intrusive thoughts family harmed wash hands 10 times fluvoxamine behavioral
questions.reviewPearl: Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP) is the specific modality of behavioral therapy proven most effective for OCD, often requiring concurrent high-dose SSRI therapy for maximum benefit.
questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of intrusive thoughts of her family being harmed. She says that the only way to stop the thoughts is to wash her hands 10 times and count backwards from 100. The thoughts occur 15 to 20 times throughout the day. She takes no medications. Physical examination shows no abnormalities except for dry, chapped hands. Mental status examination shows a mildly anxious affect. There is no evidence of hallucinations or delusions. She is alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Concentration and short- and long-term memory are intact. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder and identify the combination of an SSRI and behavioral therapy as the optimal treatment.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is suffering from severe Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD), characterized by intrusive obsessions (family being harmed) and time-consuming compulsions (hand washing, counting). The gold-standard, first-line treatment for OCD is a combination of a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI), such as fluvoxamine, and cognitive-behavioral therapy (specifically, exposure and response prevention).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Psychodynamic psychotherapy (A, C, F) lacks efficacy for the core symptoms of OCD. Diazepam (B, C, D) is a benzodiazepine that can temporarily reduce anxiety but does not treat the underlying neurobiological mechanisms of OCD and carries a risk of dependence. SSRI monotherapy (G) is less effective than combination therapy with CBT.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP) is the specific modality of behavioral therapy proven most effective for OCD, often requiring concurrent high-dose SSRI therapy for maximum benefit.

questions.retrievalTag: Binge-eating vomiting yellow dental enamel increased amylase
questions.reviewPearl: Elevated serum amylase in a young, otherwise healthy female with a normal BMI is highly suggestive of purging behavior, as chronic vomiting induces bilateral parotid gland hypertrophy and salivary amylase hypersecretion.
questions.stem: A 27-year-old woman comes to the physician for an examination prior to starting a new job. She has a 10-year history of binge-eating and self-induced vomiting. She takes no medications. She does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. She is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 72 kg (160 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. Her temperature is 37ºC (98.6ºF), pulse is 70/min, respirations are 10/min, and blood pressure is 120/70 mm Hg. Physical examination shows yellow dental enamel and abdominal striae. Serum studies are most likely to show which of the following abnormalities?
questions.educationalObjective: Correlate chronic self-induced vomiting in bulimia nervosa with physical findings (dental enamel erosion) and characteristic laboratory abnormalities (elevated salivary amylase).

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has a normal BMI, normal vital signs, and a long history of binge-eating and purging, confirming Bulimia Nervosa. Chronic self-induced vomiting leads to hyperstimulation and enlargement of the parotid (salivary) glands, which causes a prominent elevation in serum salivary amylase.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body:
Purging through vomiting results in the loss of stomach acid (HCl), causing a metabolic alkalosis with an
increased
bicarbonate concentration, not decreased (A). It also causes profound hypokalemia, not hyperkalemia (D). Magnesium and sodium levels are typically normal or decreased due to volume depletion, not increased (C, E).

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Elevated serum amylase in a young, otherwise healthy female with a normal BMI is highly suggestive of purging behavior, as chronic vomiting induces bilateral parotid gland hypertrophy and salivary amylase hypersecretion.

questions.retrievalTag: Inspects dust particular way nervous tense sertraline
questions.reviewPearl: Symptoms in OCD are ego-dystonic (distressing to the patient), whereas symptoms in OCPD are ego-syntonic (the patient views their rigid behaviors as correct and desirable). SSRIs are indicated for the former.
questions.stem: A 25-year-old man comes to the physician with his wife because of bizarre behavior for the past 3 months. She reports that he will only travel to work by taking certain routes and is overly concerned about air quality, routinely inspecting for dust on the furniture and closing the windows when he returns from work. He has organized his office and his home in a particular way and becomes extremely upset when anything is rearranged. He realizes that his demands are unreasonable but says that “I would be very nervous and tense if it were arranged any other way.” He has had no changes in sleep pattern. Physical examination shows abnormalities. On mental status examination, he reports feeling mildly anxious and depressed “all the time.” Results of laboratory studies are within reference ranges. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Differentiate OCD from OCPD based on the ego-dystonic nature of the symptoms and select an SSRI as the first-line medication.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient demonstrates obsessive thoughts (air quality, contamination) and compulsive behaviors (dust inspection, extreme organization) that he recognizes as unreasonable (ego-dystonic). This distress indicates Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD), rather than Obsessive-Compulsive Personality Disorder. The first-line pharmacotherapy for OCD is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI), such as sertraline.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Bupropion (A) and Buspirone (B) have no proven efficacy in treating the core symptoms of OCD. Clonazepam (C) is a benzodiazepine that masks anxiety but does not correct the underlying serotonin imbalance. Haloperidol (D) is an antipsychotic, which is inappropriate for primary OCD.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Symptoms in OCD are ego-dystonic (distressing to the patient), whereas symptoms in OCPD are ego-syntonic (the patient views their rigid behaviors as correct and desirable). SSRIs are indicated for the former.

questions.retrievalTag: Metastatic breast cancer mumbling Ca 12.5 hypercalcemia
questions.reviewPearl: "Stones, bones, groans, and psychiatric overtones": Always check a serum calcium level in cancer patients presenting with sudden psychiatric symptoms, confusion, or lethargy.
questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer is admitted to the hospital because of changes in her mental status during the past 3 days. Her family says she has been mumbling and softly moaning. They also report that she has been drinking more alcohol since she found out how far her disease has progressed. Current medications include fentanyl patches. There have not been any recent changes to her medication regimen. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she appears to be hallucinating. She is not oriented to person, place or time. Laboratory studies show: Hematocrit 35% Leukocyte count 8000/mm3 Serum Na+ 137 mEq/L Ca2+ 12.5 mg/dL Urea nitrogen 15 mg/dL Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL Albumin 1.5 g/dL Urinalysis shows no abnormalities. X-rays of the chest and a CT scan of the head show no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s change in mental status?

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features

questions.educationalObjective: Identify malignancy-associated hypercalcemia as a major organic cause of delirium in patients with metastatic cancer.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: This patient presents with acute delirium (hallucinations, disorientation, mumbling) in the setting of severely elevated serum calcium (12.5 mg/dL; true ionized calcium is even higher due to her profound hypoalbuminemia). Hypercalcemia of malignancy is a frequent and life-threatening cause of acute encephalopathy/delirium in patients with advanced cancers involving bone metastases (like breast cancer).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body:
While fentanyl (A) can cause altered mental status, her dose has not changed, making it less likely to be the acute trigger compared to the glaring metabolic abnormality. She is drinking
more
alcohol, meaning she is not in withdrawal (B). Meningitis (E) requires signs of infection (fever, elevated WBCs). MDD with psychotic features (D) is a psychiatric diagnosis of exclusion that does not account for the severe organic hypercalcemia.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: "Stones, bones, groans, and psychiatric overtones": Always check a serum calcium level in cancer patients presenting with sudden psychiatric symptoms, confusion, or lethargy.

questions.retrievalTag: Unaware of surroundings restricted lateral gaze gynecomastia Vitamin B1
questions.reviewPearl:
Wernicke encephalopathy is a medical emergency; intravenous thiamine must be administered immediately, and specifically
before
administering any glucose, to prevent permanent Korsakoff psychosis.
questions.stem: A 37-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by the police after they found him sitting in an alley unaware of his surroundings or situation. On arrival, he cannot remember his name, date of birth, or age. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows numerous ecchymoses over the face and extremities. Right lateral gaze is severely restricted. On left lateral gaze, there is bilateral nystagmus. There is mild gynecomastia and palmar erythema. Abdominal examination shows right upper quadrant tenderness. The liver edge is palpated 4 cm below the right costal margin. Muscle strength is normal, but he is unable to stand or walk. Deep tendon reflexes are absent. The most likely cause of these findings is a deficiency of which of the following?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize Wernicke encephalopathy by its classic triad of confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia, and identify its cause as Vitamin B1 (thiamine) deficiency.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has chronic liver disease (gynecomastia, palmar erythema, hepatomegaly) indicative of severe alcohol use disorder. He presents with Wernicke encephalopathy, defined by the classic triad of encephalopathy (unaware, amnesic), oculomotor dysfunction (restricted gaze, nystagmus), and gait ataxia (unable to walk). This condition is caused by a severe deficiency of Vitamin B1 (thiamine). His absent reflexes also indicate a concurrent thiamine-related alcoholic polyneuropathy.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Niacin (B3) deficiency (B) results in pellagra (diarrhea, dermatitis, dementia). Vitamin B12 deficiency (E) causes macrocytic anemia and subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (spasticity, loss of vibration sense), not this specific triad. Folic acid (A) deficiency causes macrocytic anemia without significant focal neurological defects.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body:
Wernicke encephalopathy is a medical emergency; intravenous thiamine must be administered immediately, and specifically
before
administering any glucose, to prevent permanent Korsakoff psychosis.

questions.retrievalTag: 16-year-old party conjunctivae injected prolonged response latency marijuana
questions.reviewPearl: Conjunctival injection is one of the most reliable and consistent physical examination findings in acute cannabis intoxication.
questions.stem: A 16-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her father because of unusual behavior since returning from a party early this morning. Her father reports that when she arrived home, she barricaded herself in her room and refused to respond to his pleas to open the door. When he entered the room 1 hour later, he found her huddled in a corner in the dark, saying that the police wanted to kill her. She has no history of medical or psychiatric illness. Her pulse is 115/min, and blood pressure is 150/90 mm Hg. The pupils are 2 mm, equal, and reactive to light and accommodation. The conjunctivae are injected. On mental status examination, she appears apprehensive but is cooperative. Her speech is slow with a prolonged response latency. She believes that she is being followed by plainclothes police officer who want to assassinate her. She hears a male voice making threatening comments. She is oriented to person and place but not to time. She can perform double-digit money calculations but not serial sevens. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose acute marijuana intoxication by recognizing physical signs (conjunctival injection, tachycardia) accompanying psychological symptoms (paranoia, slowed mentation).

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient presents with acute substance-induced psychosis marked by severe paranoia, auditory hallucinations, and delayed reaction times (prolonged response latency) following a party. The physical findings of injected conjunctivae (red eyes) and tachycardia are highly specific physiological markers for acute marijuana (cannabis) intoxication.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Cocaine intoxication (not withdrawal, C) could present with acute paranoia but would prominently feature dilated pupils, not normal 2 mm pupils. Alcohol intoxication (A) produces ataxia and disinhibition rather than florid paranoia and red eyes. Primary psychiatric illnesses like Schizophrenia (I) and Delusional disorder (D) develop over longer periods and are diagnoses of exclusion after ruling out substance intoxication.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: Conjunctival injection is one of the most reliable and consistent physical examination findings in acute cannabis intoxication.

questions.retrievalTag: ED nocturnal erections intact drinks several alcoholic beverages elevated GGT alcohol
questions.reviewPearl: The presence of normal nocturnal or morning erections reliably differentiates psychogenic or substance-induced erectile dysfunction from structural, vascular, or neurological impotence.
questions.stem: A 29-year-old man comes to the physician because of the inability to maintain an erection through completion of sexual activity. He has been married for 3 months and has not been able to complete coitus over the past 2 months. He is usually able to attain an erection by direct penile stimulation and is often aware of nocturnal erections when waking during the night. For the past month, he and his wife have attempted to have intercourse about twice a week. He drinks several alcoholic beverages before engaging in sexual activity in an effort to relax. He has had two major depressive episodes over the past 4 years and was successfully treated with desipramine; he has not taken the medication since his marriage. He has borderline hypertension but takes no medications for it. He has a strong family history of type 2 diabetes mellitus. He takes ranitidine for stomach problems. His vital signs are normal. Cranial nerve function appears normal. He walks normally. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 11 g/dL Platelet count 178,000/mm3 Serum Fasting glucose 86 mg/dL -Glutamyltransferase 70 U/L (N=5-50) Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify alcohol intoxication as a prominent secondary cause of situational erectile dysfunction, especially when supported by elevated liver enzymes (GGT).

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: This patient's erectile dysfunction is situational; the preservation of nocturnal erections and erections via direct stimulation confirms that his neurovascular pathways are completely intact. His practice of drinking "several alcoholic beverages" before sex to relax impairs his physiological sexual performance. His elevated -Glutamyltransferase (GGT) strongly corroborates a history of heavy alcohol use, cementing it as the direct pharmacological cause of his ED.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: He is no longer taking desipramine (B), which rules it out. His fasting glucose is completely normal, ruling out Diabetes mellitus (C). While performance anxiety might prompt his drinking, the heavy acute consumption of a CNS depressant (alcohol) immediately before intercourse is the dominant physiological block. Ranitidine (G) is highly unlikely to cause this pattern compared to his alcohol use.

explanationSections.heading: Clinical Pearl
body: The presence of normal nocturnal or morning erections reliably differentiates psychogenic or substance-induced erectile dysfunction from structural, vascular, or neurological impotence.
